name: Kortum, Philip
recent_1: Kortum, P., Ziegler Acemyan, C., & Oswald, F. L. (2026). System Usability Measured Over Time: An Exploratory Descriptive Study. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2026.2630077
recent_1_synopsis: An exploratory, descriptive investigation of how System Usability Scale (SUS) ratings for the same systems change across repeated measurements over time. The study extends Kortum's long-running program of SUS measurement work by examining the temporal stability and drift of usability judgments, an underexplored psychometric property of the scale.
recent_2: Diokno, A., Kim, N., Kortum, P., & Byrne, M. (2024). Emergence of Paper-Digital Systems: A Usability Evaluation of Single-Page Versus Multipage Digital Instructions in a Ballot Mailing Task. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813241260679
recent_2_synopsis: An empirical usability study comparing single-page versus multipage digital instruction formats for a mail-ballot task, situated in Kortum's voting-systems research line. The work evaluates how the design of paper-digital hybrid instructions affects task performance and error, with direct implications for election usability and accessibility.
recent_3: Asma, E., Heenan, M., Banda, G., ... Kortum, P., Kawaza, K., Oden, M., Richards-Kortum, R. (2023). Avoid equipment graveyards: rigorous process to improve identification and procurement of effective, affordable, and usable newborn devices in low-resource hospital settings. BMC Pediatrics, 23(Suppl 2), 569. DOI: 10.1186/s12887-023-04362-x
recent_3_synopsis: Describes a rigorous, usability-informed process for selecting and procuring neonatal medical devices that can survive the constraints of low-resource hospital settings (power instability, dust, high user turnover, maintenance burden). Kortum contributes the human-factors/usability-assessment perspective to a global-health device-procurement framework, reflecting his applied work on usable systems in global health.
seminal_1: Bangor, A., Kortum, P., & Miller, J. (2009). Determining what individual SUS scores mean: Adding an adjective rating scale. Journal of Usability Studies, 4(3), 114-123.
seminal_1_synopsis: Introduced an adjective rating scale anchored to SUS scores, giving practitioners an interpretable, plain-language mapping (e.g., 'good,' 'excellent') for otherwise abstract 0-100 SUS values. With roughly 7,700 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026) it is among the most-cited usability-measurement papers ever published and is foundational because it made the SUS directly actionable for design teams and benchmarking. (No Crossref DOI: the Journal of Usability Studies did not register DOIs for this issue; flagged in notes.)
seminal_2: Bangor, A., Kortum, P. T., & Miller, J. T. (2008). An Empirical Evaluation of the System Usability Scale. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 24(6), 574-594. DOI: 10.1080/10447310802205776
seminal_2_synopsis: Reports nearly ten years of SUS data across hundreds of products and study phases, establishing normative benchmarks and demonstrating the scale's reliability, validity, and sensitivity. With roughly 7,100 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026) it is the empirical cornerstone that legitimized the SUS as the de facto standard usability questionnaire and provided the reference data the field still uses to interpret scores.
expertise_overview: Kortum's research centers on the measurement of usability and user experience, with deep methodological focus on the System Usability Scale (SUS), its psychometrics, scoring, interpretation, and cross-language/cross-domain validity. He develops and refines standardized usability and trust metrics and applies them to design and evaluation of highly usable systems. His primary application domains are voting and election systems (ballot usability, security/usability tradeoffs, e2e-verifiable voting), mobile and smartphone computing, and global-health medical devices for low-resource settings. Methodologically he favors empirical, quantitative usability assessment, survey instrument validation, normative benchmarking, and controlled user studies of task performance and error. He also engages broadly with human factors topics such as warning design, interface controls, and the societal role of human factors in democracy.
notes: Identity confidence: HIGH. Confirmed Philip (Ted) Kortum, Professor, Department of Psychological Sciences, Rice University; Google Scholar profile user=BD7BLDgAAAAJ (~24,369 total cites, h-index 49). Research focus on SUS/usability measurement, voting systems, mobile computing, and global-health devices matches the seed person exactly. Sources: Google Scholar profile, Rice University faculty profile/news, ResearchGate, SAGE/Taylor&Francis publisher pages, PubMed/BMC. Citation counts from Google Scholar, accessed June 2026 (approximate, rounded). DOI verification: recent_1 (10.1080/10447318.2026.2630077), recent_2 (10.1177/10711813241260679), recent_3 (10.1186/s12887-023-04362-x), and seminal_2 (10.1080/10447310802205776) all verified 200 + matching title via Crossref API. UNVERIFIABLE DOI: seminal_1 (Bangor, Kortum & Miller 2009, Journal of Usability Studies) has no registered Crossref DOI; included without DOI per instructions and flagged here. All hf_papers DOIs treated as pre-verified.
